data.sentence: Okay, I think everyone's on now — Tyler, can you hear us okay?
Yeah, yeah I'm here, sorry I was — had to grab the right headset, my other one died.
Alright, let's just get into it because honestly we've been in firefighting mode for the past four days and I want to actually understand what happened here.
Yeah, agreed. So, Mike, do you want to walk us through what you found? You were the one digging into the pipeline logs last night.

Sure, yeah. So, um, the short version is — we had a cascading failure in the event ingestion layer. There's essentially a single point of failure in how we're routing events through the processing pipeline, and once that node went down on Monday morning, everything backed up.
Wait, a single point of failure? Like, we've known about that risk for how long? I feel like that was flagged in Q3 last year.
It was flagged, yeah. It was in the backlog. It just... never got prioritized above the feature work.

That is honestly really frustrating to hear, Mike, because we had six hours — six hours — where customers had zero threat monitoring visibility. Like, Detect is supposed to be the thing that tells you when something bad is happening.
I know. I know, and I'm not trying to make excuses. The prioritization call was made above our level and at the time there were reasons, but obviously in hindsight—
In hindsight it was the wrong call, yeah.
Can we talk about the actual failure sequence though? Like I want to understand technically what cascaded. Was it a memory issue, was it network, what actually triggered the initial node failure?

So from what I can tell in the logs, the primary ingestion node started throwing out-of-memory errors around 6:47 AM. And instead of failing gracefully, it just — it locked up. And then the queue started building, and because there was no circuit breaker, the downstream processors kept trying to pull from a source that wasn't responding.
Right, and that's what caused the cascading part. The downstream processors themselves started degrading.
Exactly. And by the time our alerting caught it — which was, ugh, almost 40 minutes later — the whole pipeline was basically dead.
Forty minutes before alerting kicked in? Why did it take that long? We have health checks on those services.

The health checks were pinging the node endpoint, which was still technically responding — it just wasn't processing anything. So the check was returning healthy even though the service was completely stuck.
Oh, that's — okay, so the health check was essentially useless in this scenario.
Pretty much, yeah. It was a liveness check, not a readiness check. Which is a gap we clearly need to fix.
Okay so we've got at least three things here — the single point of failure in ingestion, no circuit breaker pattern, and inadequate health checking. Is that a fair summary so far?
Yeah, that's how I'd characterize it. And I think the fix for the ingestion layer specifically is redundant processing nodes — at minimum two, probably three, with active load balancing.
And the circuit breaker — like, how complicated is that to implement? I don't want us to say 'we'll implement a circuit breaker' and then that also ends up sitting in the backlog for a year.
It's not trivial but it's also not like a three-month project. I think we could have a working implementation in two, maybe two and a half weeks if we actually focus on it.
I want that estimate in writing honestly, because I've heard 'two weeks' before and then it becomes six.
That's fair, Nina, that's fair. I'll put together a proper scope doc today.
What about the communication side of this? Like, customers were in the dark for hours. Do we know how that was handled?
That's — yeah, that's a whole other conversation, but I will say the status page update was way too slow. That's something that needs to be escalated separately.

Agreed. And from a QA perspective, I'm also really bothered by the fact that we didn't have a test scenario for this kind of failure. Like, do we even have chaos engineering coverage on the ingestion pipeline?
We don't. Not really. We have some basic load testing but nothing that simulates a node going unresponsive.
Yeah, that needs to change. Like, we should be deliberately killing nodes in staging on a regular basis to validate that the system handles it.
One hundred percent. I'm going to push for that as part of the remediation plan. It won't just be 'fix the code' — it needs to be 'build the testing practice too.'
Good. And honestly, uh, I think we need to be really transparent in the post-mortem doc about the fact that this risk was known and deprioritized. I don't think we should soften that.
I agree with that. If we're going to learn from this, we have to be honest about the decision-making chain that got us here, not just the technical failure.
Yeah. It's uncomfortable but it's the right call.

Okay, so action items — Mike is doing the scope doc for the circuit breaker and redundant nodes, Ananya and Tyler are looking at the health check improvements, and Nina, can you start scoping out what a chaos testing framework would look like for the pipeline?
Yeah, I can do that. I'll probably want to pull in Tyler for part of it.
Works for me.

Alright. This has been a really rough week and I don't think anyone feels great about where we're at, but — let's make sure this doesn't happen again. I'll circulate the post-mortem draft by end of day tomorrow.
Thanks everyone.
